macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek 7: The Institution of Zimun and Overview of the Mishnah

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and structural overview of Perek 7 on Birkat Hamazon and Zimun
The essence and purpose of Zimun: calling people to bless as a group rather than individually
First Mishnah: Three people who ate together are obligated in Zimun
Explanation of Demai and its status as quasi-forbidden food
Contrast between permitted foods and forbidden foods regarding Zimun participation
Discussion of economic and market implications of Demai regulations
Discussion of women, slaves, children, and minimum quantities for Zimun

macro_segments.title: Biblical and Textual Foundations for the Three-Person Zimun Requirement

micro_segments.title: Rav Assi's derivation of Zimun from the verse 'Gadlu Lashem iti' emphasizing calling others and unified praise
Rabbi Abbahu's alternative source from 'Ki Shem Hashem Ekra' showing the call-and-response structure
Analysis of the institutional nature of Zimun and connection to public prayer
Discussion of why Zimun requires three while Hamotzei does not, including Tosafot's distinction between meal initiation and conclusion

macro_segments.title: Nuanced Applications of Zimun: Sharing and Benefit in Mixed-Dietary Situations

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's analysis: Can Zimun occur when one person eats food another cannot, such as Kohen eating Terumah
Yerushalmi principle: Zimun is valid if at least one person can eat from both parties' food
Vow-based restrictions: If neither party can derive benefit from the other, Zimun is impossible
Modern applications: Meat and milk separation, Pas Akum stringencies, and who leads Zimun
Question about non-halachic barriers (medical, celiac) to shared eating and Zimun participation

macro_segments.title: The Voice Hierarchy in Blessing Responses and Translations: Equal Dignity in Joint Blessing

micro_segments.title: Rav Chana bar Abba: Person saying Amein should not raise voice louder than the one making blessing
Rabbi Shimon ben Pazi: The Metargem (Aramaic translator) should not exceed the volume of the Torah reader
Complex source analysis: Moshe speaking and God responding—textual crux and Tosafot's explanation
Teaching in Baraita: When impossible for Metargem to match reader's volume, the reader lowers voice to equalize
Significance of equality: Maintaining the dignity of translation and joint participation in blessing

macro_segments.title: The Case of Two People Eating Together: Shomea Ke'oneh vs. True Zimun

micro_segments.title: Debate between Rav and Rabbi Yochanan on whether two people can optionally perform Zimun
Three people cannot split into two groups to each perform separate Zimun
The case of a servant eating with two people: implicit permission from obligation to Zimun

macro_segments.title: Women, Slaves, and Children in Zimun: Obligation, Propriety, and Group Composition

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Women and slaves can perform Zimun among themselves but cannot form mixed-gender groups
Rashi's reading: 'Hundred women equal two men'—women are in the optional (Reshut) category, not obligatory
Tosafot's alternative: Women may actually be obligated in Zimun, not merely permitted
Broader question: If women are obligated in Birkat Hamazon, why exclude them from mixed-gender Zimun?
Concern about Pritzuta (impropriety): reason for prohibition against mixed-gender Zimun

macro_segments.title: The Case of Two People Eating Together: Shomea Ke'oneh vs. True Zimun (continued)

micro_segments.title: Proof that two can only be Shomea Ke'oneh (piggybacking), not true Zimun institution
Resolution: Rav permits two as optional, Rabbi Yochanan does not; testimony from rabbis from the West

topical_tags.term: Rav vs. Rabbi Yochanan (dispute on two-person Zimun)
Milchigs and Fleishigs (מילכיגס פליישיגס - dairy and meat)
